title: Data Types, Populations vs. Samples, Levels of Measurement

questions.explanation: The four levels stack: nominal adds categories, ordinal adds order, interval adds equal differences, and ratio adds a true zero. Only with a true zero do ratios make sense — $200 is twice $100 because $0 means none at all, whereas 80°F is not twice 40°F because 0°F is an arbitrary point.

A 1–5 rating has a meaningful order (5 beats 4) but no guarantee of equal gaps — the jump from 4 to 5 need not equal the jump from 2 to 3. Order without equal intervals is the definition of ordinal. Interval would require the differences themselves to be meaningful quantities.

The dividing line is what the number was computed from, not what it describes. 8.4% came from the 120 sampled invoices, so it is a statistic (p̂) — a different random 120 would give a different value. The parameter is the unknown error rate p among all 9,400 invoices that p̂ estimates.

Picking a random start and then every k-th case (here k = 25) is systematic sampling. Stratified would divide callers into groups and sample within each; cluster would take whole groups; voluntary response would let callers opt in themselves.

Proportional allocation gives each stratum its population share of the sample: n_h = n × N_h / N = 300 × 1,500 / 9,000 = 300 × 1/6 = 50 Platinum members. (Silver gets 150 and Gold gets 100 by the same rule.)
The systematic interval is k = N / n = 12,000 / 400 = 30: choose a random start among the first 30 units, then inspect every 30th unit thereafter, yielding exactly 400 inspections across the run.

When respondents select themselves, those with strong (usually negative) feelings dominate — voluntary response bias. Because the error is systematic, not random, collecting more self-selected responses does not fix it; it just estimates the wrong number more precisely (the Literary Digest lesson). Sample size cures sampling error, never bias.

The sampling frame is the concrete list you actually draw from (the loyalty database, a phone directory). Gaps between the frame and the true population — customers not in the loyalty program — are a built-in source of selection bias before a single case is drawn.
